MyPrograms__Animation.py: A sphere starts at the origin and moves counterclockwise around a circle of radius 5, leaving a trail of points. A separate graph plots its x and y coordinates against time as green and magenta dots. After one full revolution the program prints 'Animation over!'
MyPrograms__MakingShapes.py: Draw a simple table on a white background. The tabletop is a black box positioned off-center, with its length, width, and height set by variables. A single cylinder of the same color extends downward from the center of the tabletop as the leg, with radius one tenth of the table width.
MyPrograms__Orbits.py: A small planet orbits a stationary orange sun in a circle of radius 5, leaving a trail of points. Thin cylinders mark the x and y axes across the scene. User spin is disabled, the field of view is very narrow, and the background is white. The planet advances around the circle inside a while loop until it completes one revolution.
MyPrograms__PlanetaryMotion.py: Model three planets and a massive central star interacting through Newtonian gravity. Each body is a sphere with its own mass, momentum, and color, and every body leaves a trail. A function computes the gravitational force between any two bodies. In an infinite loop with a very small time step, sum the forces on each body from all the others, update each body's momentum from its net force, then update each position from its momentum.
MyPrograms__ProjectileMotion1Dim.py: A red ball falls from height 10 toward a ground plane, with quadratic air drag opposing gravity. Positions and velocities advance with a midpoint integration scheme inside a while loop that runs until the ball reaches the ground. The ball leaves a point trail, a graph plots vertical velocity against time, and the program prints the landing time and landing speed. The scene has a cyan background.
MyPrograms__ProjectileMotion2Dim.py: A red ball is launched at 45 degrees with speed 10 over a ground plane, with quadratic air drag acting in both x and y. A 'Launch!' button prints a message when clicked. Positions and velocities advance with a midpoint integration scheme, the ball leaves a point trail on a cyan background, and the loop stops updating once the ball falls below the ground.
